text: Before electricity, the boundary between work and rest was enforced by darkness. The sociologist Max Weber traced how the Protestant work ethic began eroding leisure as a moral category, but even Weber could not have imagined a technology that makes every waking hour equally available for labor. Researcher Arlie Hochschild found that for many dual-income households, email and mobile phones had effectively abolished the 'third shift' — the small pockets of personal time — by making it permanently interruptible. What is lost is not merely rest but the psychological condition that rest requires: the genuine belief that one is, for now, allowed to stop.
question: What does Hochschild mean by the abolition of the 'third shift'?

options.text: The elimination of overnight work schedules in knowledge industries
The disappearance of domestic labor as a recognized form of work
The loss of small pockets of personal time made permanently interruptible by mobile technology
The merging of commute time with productive working hours through podcasts
